definition: Assalamualaikum! I'm CS Genie Pro – your AI tutor for Hacking, IT, and all of Computer Science!
basic_answers: Assalamualaikum! I'm CS Genie Pro – your AI tutor for Hacking, IT, and all of Computer Science!
I greet with: Assalamualaikum! I'm CS Genie Pro – your AI tutor for Hacking, IT, and all of Computer Science!
My intro is: Assalamualaikum! I'm CS Genie Pro – your AI tutor for Hacking, IT, and all of Computer Science!

definition: Goodbye! Keep exploring and keep learning!
basic_questions: What do you say at the end of a session?
basic_answers: I say: Goodbye! Keep exploring and keep learning!
My farewell message is: Goodbye! Keep exploring and keep learning!

definition: Every expert was once a beginner. Code daily, break things, learn, repeat!
basic_questions: How can I stay motivated in CS?
basic_answers: Every expert was once a beginner. Code daily, break things, learn, repeat! 💻
Remember: Every expert was once a beginner. Code daily, break things, learn, repeat!

definition: An algorithm is a finite sequence of well-defined, step-by-step instructions to solve a computational problem or perform a task.
basic_questions: Give an example of an algorithm in daily life.
basic_answers: An algorithm is a step-by-step recipe to solve a problem, like a cooking recipe but for computers.
Example: To make tea → 1. Boil water, 2. Add tea leaves, 3. Add sugar, 4. Add milk, 5. Serve.
Algorithms make computers solve problems efficiently and correctly.

definition: Time complexity measures the amount of computational time an algorithm takes as a function of input size, typically expressed in Big O notation.
basic_answers: Time complexity tells how fast an algorithm runs as input grows. Measured in Big O.
O(n) means time grows linearly — if input doubles, time roughly doubles.
It helps choose fast algorithms for large data.

definition: Space complexity refers to the amount of memory an algorithm uses relative to the input size.
basic_questions: Give an example of high space complexity.
basic_answers: Space complexity measures how much memory (RAM) an algorithm needs.
Creating a copy of the entire array → O(n) space.
O(1) means constant memory — uses same memory no matter input size.

definition: Big O notation describes the upper bound of an algorithm's growth rate, focusing on worst-case performance.
basic_questions: What’s the difference between O(n) and O(n²)?
basic_answers: Big O describes how slow an algorithm gets as input grows — worst case.
O(n): linear (fast), O(n²): quadratic (slow for large n).
Yes! O(1) is constant time — fastest possible.

definition: Omega notation describes the lower bound of an algorithm's growth rate in the best case.
basic_answers: Omega (Ω) gives the best-case lower bound — minimum time an algorithm will take.
Ω(n) means at best, it takes time proportional to n.

definition: Theta notation provides a tight bound, meaning both upper and lower bounds are the same.
basic_answers: Theta (Θ) means tight bound — both best and worst case are the same.
Use Θ when upper (O) and lower (Ω) bounds match, e.g., merge sort is Θ(n log n).

definition: A fixed-size collection of elements of the same type stored in contiguous memory locations.
basic_questions: How do you access array elements?
basic_answers: An array is a list of items of the same type stored in continuous memory.
Use index: arr[0] = first element, arr[1] = second, etc.
Index is the position number, starting from 0.

definition: An array that automatically resizes when full, used in languages like Python (list) and Java (ArrayList).
basic_questions: Python list is what kind of array?
basic_answers: An array that grows/shrinks automatically when you add/remove items.
Python list = dynamic array.
No need to specify size upfront — flexible and easy to use.

definition: A linear data structure where each element (node) points to the next, allowing dynamic size and efficient insertions.
basic_questions: Difference between array and linked list?
basic_answers: A chain of nodes where each node holds data and pointer to next node.
Array: fixed size, fast access. Linked List: dynamic, fast insert/delete.
Insert/delete in middle is O(1) if you have the node.

definition: A linked list where each node has pointers to both next and previous nodes.
basic_questions: What is a doubly linked list?
Why use doubly linked list over singly?
basic_answers: Each node has data, next pointer, and previous pointer.
Can traverse backward easily — useful in browser history, music playlist.

definition: A linked list where the last node points back to the first, forming a circle.
basic_questions: What is a circular linked list?
Use case of circular linked list?
basic_answers: Last node points to first — no start/end, forms a loop.
Round-robin scheduling, music playlist loop, game turns.

definition: A LIFO (Last In, First Out) data structure supporting push, pop, and peek operations.
basic_answers: LIFO: Last In, First Out — like a stack of plates.
Undo (Ctrl+Z), function call stack, browser back button.
Push (add), Pop (remove), Peek (view top).

definition: A FIFO (First In, First Out) data structure used in scheduling, breadth-first search, and buffering.
basic_questions: Example of queue in real life?
basic_answers: FIFO: First In, First Out — like a line at a bank.
Printer queue, CPU task scheduling, message queue.
Enqueue (add), Dequeue (remove), Front (view first).

definition: Double-ended queue allowing insertion and deletion from both ends.
basic_answers: Double-ended queue — add/remove from front or back.
Sliding window, palindrome check, browser history.

definition: A queue where elements are served based on priority, implemented using heaps.
basic_questions: How is it different from normal queue?
basic_answers: Queue where higher priority items come out first (not FIFO).
Normal queue: FIFO. Priority queue: highest priority first (e.g., hospital emergency).

definition: A complete binary tree satisfying the heap property: parent ≥ children (max-heap) or parent ≤ children (min-heap).
basic_answers: Complete binary tree where parent is greater/less than children.
Min-heap: parent ≤ children. Max-heap: parent ≥ children.
Priority queue, heap sort, scheduling.

definition: A tree where each node has at most two children: left and right.
basic_questions: How many children can a node have?
basic_answers: Tree where each node has at most 2 children: left and right.
Maximum 2 children per node.

definition: A binary tree where left subtree < node < right subtree, enabling O(log n) search.
basic_questions: Why is search fast in BST?
basic_answers: Binary tree where left < node < right.
Search halves the tree each time → O(log n).

definition: A self-balancing BST where height difference between subtrees ≤ 1.
basic_answers: Self-balancing BST — keeps height difference ≤ 1.
Rotations keep tree balanced → O(log n) always.

definition: A self-balancing BST using color bits to ensure O(log n) operations.
basic_answers: Self-balancing BST with red/black colors to maintain balance.
Used in Java (TreeMap), C++ STL (map), Linux kernel.

definition: A self-balancing tree designed for disk-based storage, used in databases and file systems.
basic_answers: Multi-way balanced tree with many children per node.
Minimizes disk reads — perfect for databases.

definition: A variant of B-tree where all values are stored in leaves, used in database indexing.
basic_answers: B-tree variant: data only in leaves, internal nodes have keys.
Faster range queries, better for indexing.

definition: A prefix tree used for efficient string retrieval, autocomplete, and spell checking.
basic_answers: Tree where each node is a character, paths form words.
Autocomplete, spell check, dictionary.

definition: A compressed trie containing all suffixes of a string, used in text processing.
basic_answers: Compressed trie of all suffixes of a string.
Substring search, longest common substring.

definition: A data structure using hash functions to map keys to array indices for O(1) average lookup.
basic_answers: Data structure that maps keys to values using hash function.
Direct index access via hash → average O(1).
Python dict = hash table.

definition: A function that converts a key into an array index. Good hash functions minimize collisions.
basic_answers: Function that turns key (e.g., 'apple') into array index (e.g., 3).
Two keys hash to same index.

definition: Techniques to handle when two keys hash to the same index: chaining or open addressing.
basic_answers: Use chaining (linked list) or open addressing (probing).
1. Chaining, 2. Open Addressing.

definition: Collision resolution using linked lists at each hash table index.
basic_questions: What is chaining in hash tables?
basic_answers: Store colliding items in a linked list at that index.

definition: Collision resolution by probing for the next empty slot (linear, quadratic, double hashing).
basic_answers: Find next empty slot when collision occurs.
Linear, Quadratic, Double Hashing.

definition: Open addressing where next slot is checked sequentially.
basic_answers: Check next slot: index, index+1, index+2...
Clustering — items clump together.

definition: Probing using quadratic intervals to reduce clustering.
basic_answers: Probe: index, index+1², index+2², etc. Reduces clustering.

definition: Using two hash functions to determine probe sequence.
basic_answers: Use second hash to decide step size → better distribution.

definition: A collection of nodes (vertices) connected by edges, representing relationships.
basic_answers: Nodes (vertices) + edges (connections).
Social network: people (nodes), friends (edges).

definition: A graph where edges have direction (one-way).
basic_answers: Edges have direction: A → B (not B → A).
Web pages (links), Twitter follows.

definition: A graph where edges have no direction (bidirectional).
basic_answers: Edges go both ways: A — B = B — A.
Facebook friends, road network.

definition: A graph where edges have numerical weights or costs.
basic_answers: Edges have costs (e.g., distance, time).
Shortest path (Dijkstra), flight routes.

definition: A 2D array representing graph: matrix[i][j] = 1 if edge exists between i and j.
basic_answers: 2D array: 1 if edge, 0 if no edge.
Dense graphs, fast edge check O(1).

definition: An array of lists where each index holds neighbors of a vertex.
basic_answers: Array of lists: arr[0] = [1,2], arr[1] = [0,3].
Saves space in sparse graphs.

definition: Graph traversal starting from root, exploring level by level using a queue.
basic_answers: Level-by-level traversal using queue.
Shortest path in unweighted graph, connected components.

definition: Graph traversal exploring as far as possible along each branch before backtracking.
basic_answers: Go deep first, backtrack when stuck.
Cycle detection, topological sort, maze solving.

definition: Finds shortest paths from a source to all vertices in a weighted graph with non-negative weights.
basic_answers: Finds shortest path in weighted graph (non-negative weights).
Cannot handle negative weights.

definition: Computes shortest paths in weighted graphs, handles negative weights, detects negative cycles.
basic_answers: Shortest path with negative weights, detects negative cycles.
Handles negative edges (Dijkstra cannot).

definition: Finds shortest paths between all pairs of vertices using dynamic programming.
basic_answers: All-pairs shortest path using DP.
O(V³) — good for dense graphs.

definition: Finds Minimum Spanning Tree using Union-Find and edge sorting.
basic_answers: Builds MST by adding cheapest edges without cycles.
1. Sort edges, 2. Add if no cycle (Union-Find).

definition: Builds MST by growing from a starting vertex, always choosing the smallest edge to a new vertex.
basic_answers: Grows MST from one vertex, picks min edge to new vertex.
Prim: grows from vertex. Kruskal: sorts all edges.

definition: Linear ordering of vertices in a DAG such that for every directed edge u→v, u comes before v.
basic_answers: Order tasks where A must come before B.
Course schedule, build systems, dependency resolution.

definition: A subgraph where every vertex is reachable from every other vertex.
basic_answers: Part of graph where all nodes reach each other.
Kosaraju or Tarjan’s algorithm.

definition: A vertex whose removal increases the number of connected components.
basic_answers: Node whose removal disconnects graph.
Cut vertex.

definition: An edge whose removal increases the number of connected components.
basic_questions: What is a bridge in graph?
basic_answers: Edge whose removal disconnects the graph.

definition: Efficient search in sorted array by repeatedly dividing search interval in half.
basic_answers: Search by halving sorted array each step.
O(log n).
Array must be sorted.

definition: Sequential search through each element until target is found.
basic_answers: Check each element one by one.
O(n) — worst case checks all.

definition: Repeatedly swaps adjacent elements if out of order. O(n²).
basic_answers: Swap adjacent if wrong order, bubble largest to end.
O(n²) — slow.

definition: Finds minimum element in unsorted portion and places it at the beginning.
basic_answers: Find smallest, swap with first position, repeat.

definition: Builds sorted array one item at a time by inserting into correct position.
basic_answers: Insert each element into its correct position in sorted part.
Nearly sorted arrays.

definition: Divide-and-conquer: splits array, recursively sorts, then merges. O(n log n).
basic_answers: Divide array, sort halves, merge.
O(n log n) — stable and consistent.

definition: Chooses pivot, partitions array, recursively sorts subarrays. Average O(n log n).
basic_answers: Pick pivot, partition, recurse.
O(n log n) average, O(n²) worst.

definition: Converts array to max-heap, repeatedly extracts max and heapifies.
basic_answers: Build max-heap, remove max repeatedly.

definition: Non-comparison sort for integers with known range. O(n + k).
basic_answers: When numbers are in small range (e.g., 1 to 100).

definition: Sorts integers by processing digits from least to most significant.
basic_answers: Sort digit by digit, starting from least significant.

definition: Distributes elements into buckets, sorts buckets, then concatenates.
basic_answers: Put numbers in buckets, sort each, combine.

definition: A function calling itself with a smaller input to solve subproblems.
basic_answers: Function calls itself with smaller input.
Factorial: fact(n) = n * fact(n-1)

definition: Recursion where the recursive call is the last operation, optimizable by compilers.
basic_answers: Recursive call is the last thing — can be optimized to loop.

definition: Caching results of expensive function calls to avoid recomputation.
basic_answers: Store results of function calls to reuse (e.g., Fibonacci).

definition: Solving complex problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems and storing results.
basic_answers: Solve subproblems once, store answers.
Fibonacci, Knapsack, Longest Common Subsequence.

definition: Makes locally optimal choice at each step hoping to find global optimum.
basic_answers: Choose best option now, hope for global best.
Coin change (if greedy works), Activity selection.

definition: Systematically tries all possibilities, undoing choices when a path fails.
basic_answers: Try all options, undo if wrong (e.g., N-Queens, Sudoku).

definition: Breaks problem into smaller subproblems, solves independently, combines results.
basic_answers: Divide → Conquer → Combine (e.g., merge sort).

definition: Trying all possible solutions without optimization.
basic_answers: Try every possibility (slow but correct).

definition: Directly operating on binary representations using bitwise operators.
basic_answers: Use & | ^ ~ << >> on binary (fast, low-level).

definition: Technique using two indices moving toward each other to solve array problems efficiently.
basic_answers: Left and right pointers move based on condition (e.g., two sum).

definition: Maintains a window of elements that expands or contracts to meet conditions.
basic_answers: Fixed or dynamic window over array (e.g., max subarray sum).

definition: Programming paradigm based on objects containing data and methods.
basic_answers: Programming using objects (data + functions).
Encapsulation, Abstraction, Inheritance, Polymorphism.

definition: A blueprint for creating objects with properties and behaviors.
basic_answers: Template for objects (e.g., class Car { color, speed }).

definition: An instance of a class with actual values.
basic_answers: Real instance: Car myCar = new Car();

definition: Mechanism where a new class derives properties and methods from an existing class.
basic_answers: Child class inherits from parent (code reuse).

definition: Ability of different classes to be treated as instances of the same class through inheritance.
basic_answers: Same method, different behavior (e.g., override).

definition: Bundling data and methods that operate on it within a single unit (class).
basic_answers: Hide data, expose via methods (private + getters/setters).

definition: Hiding complex implementation details and showing only essential features.
basic_answers: Show what, hide how (e.g., API, abstract class).

definition: A contract specifying what methods a class must implement.
basic_answers: 100% abstract — defines what, not how.

definition: A class that cannot be instantiated and may contain abstract methods.
basic_questions: Difference between abstract class and interface?
basic_answers: Abstract class: partial impl. Interface: no impl (before Java 8).

definition: Special method called when an object is created to initialize its state.
basic_answers: Runs when object is created: Car() { color = 'red'; }

definition: Method called when an object is destroyed to release resources.
basic_answers: Cleanup: ~Car() { free memory } (C++).

definition: Refers to the current instance of the class.
basic_answers: Refers to current object: this.name = name;

definition: Refers to the parent class in inheritance.
basic_answers: Call parent method: super.eat();

definition: Belongs to the class rather than instances; shared across all objects.
basic_answers: Shared by all objects: static int count;

definition: Prevents method overriding, variable reassignment, or class inheritance.
basic_answers: final variable: constant, final method: no override, final class: no extend.

definition: An organized collection of structured data stored electronically.
basic_answers: Organized storage of data (e.g., student records).

definition: Relational Database Management System using tables with rows and columns.
basic_answers: Database using tables, rows, columns, relationships.
MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server.

definition: Structured Query Language for managing and querying relational databases.
basic_answers: Language to talk to databases.
SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE.

definition: Non-relational databases for unstructured or semi-structured data.
basic_answers: Non-tabular databases for big/unstructured data.
Document (MongoDB), Key-Value (Redis), Graph (Neo4j).

definition: A unique identifier for each record in a table.
basic_answers: Unique ID for each row (e.g., roll_no).

definition: A field that refers to the primary key in another table.
basic_answers: Links two tables (e.g., student.dept_id → department.id).

definition: A primary key made of two or more columns.
basic_answers: Primary key from multiple columns (e.g., order_id + product_id).

definition: A minimal set of attributes that can uniquely identify a record.
basic_answers: Any column(s) that can be primary key.

definition: Process of organizing data to reduce redundancy and improve integrity.
basic_answers: Remove duplicates, split tables for clean data.

definition: First Normal Form: eliminates repeating groups, ensures atomic values.
basic_answers: No repeating groups, each cell one value.

definition: Second Normal Form: 1NF + no partial dependency on composite key.
basic_answers: 1NF + non-key depends on whole composite key.

definition: Third Normal Form: 2NF + no transitive dependency.
basic_answers: No column depends on another non-key column.

definition: Boyce-Codd Normal Form: stricter version of 3NF.
basic_answers: Stricter 3NF — every determinant is a candidate key.

definition: Combines rows from two or more tables based on related columns.
basic_answers: Combine data from multiple tables.

definition: Returns rows with matching values in both tables.
basic_answers: Only matching rows from both tables.

definition: Returns all rows from left table, matched rows from right.
basic_answers: All from left, matching from right (NULL if no match).

definition: Returns all rows from right table, matched from left.
basic_answers: All from right, matching from left.

definition: Returns all rows when there is a match in either table.
basic_answers: All rows from both tables, NULL where no match.

definition: A database structure that improves query performance on specific columns.
basic_answers: Speeds up SELECT on a column (like book index).

definition: Determines the physical order of data in a table.
basic_answers: Data physically sorted by index (one per table).

definition: A separate structure with pointers to data rows.
basic_answers: Separate index with pointers to actual data.

definition: A unit of work performed against a database as a single logical operation.
basic_answers: Group of SQL commands that succeed or fail together.

definition: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability – properties of reliable transactions.
basic_answers: A: All or nothing, C: Valid state, I: No interference, D: Permanent.

definition: Undo changes made during a transaction if it fails.
basic_answers: Undo all changes in a transaction.

definition: Save all changes made during a transaction permanently.
basic_answers: Save transaction changes permanently.

definition: Two or more transactions permanently blocked waiting for each other.
basic_answers: T1 waits for T2, T2 waits for T1 → stuck forever.

definition: Mechanisms to ensure correct results for concurrent operations.
basic_answers: Manage multiple transactions without conflict.

definition: Software that manages hardware, software resources, and provides services for programs.
basic_answers: Middleman between user and hardware.
Windows, Linux, macOS, Android.

definition: Core component of OS that controls hardware and system operations.
basic_answers: Heart of OS — talks to CPU, memory, devices.

definition: An executing program with its own memory space and system resources.
basic_answers: Running program with its own memory.

definition: Lightweight process sharing memory with other threads in the same process.
basic_answers: Process: own memory. Thread: shares memory with others.

definition: Executing multiple threads concurrently within a single process.
basic_answers: Run multiple threads in one process (faster context switch).

definition: Determining which process gets the CPU when multiple are ready.
basic_answers: Decide which process runs next on CPU.

definition: First Come First Served scheduling: non-preemptive, simple but long waiting time.
basic_answers: First arrived, first served — like a queue.

definition: Shortest Job First: selects process with smallest execution time.
basic_answers: Run shortest job first — minimizes wait time.

definition: Preemptive scheduling with fixed time quantum for fairness.
basic_answers: Each process gets fixed time slice (quantum) in cycle.

definition: OS component that controls and coordinates main memory.
basic_answers: Allocate/deallocate memory to processes.

definition: Technique allowing execution of processes not completely in memory using disk.
basic_answers: Use disk as extra RAM — run big programs.

definition: Divides memory into fixed-size pages and frames.
basic_answers: Split memory into fixed pages (4KB) and frames.

definition: Occurs when a requested page is not in memory and must be loaded from disk.
basic_answers: Page not in RAM → load from disk (slow).

definition: Excessive paging that degrades system performance.
basic_answers: Too much paging → CPU busy swapping, no work done.

definition: Method and data structure OS uses to control file storage and retrieval.
basic_answers: How OS stores and organizes files on disk.

definition: File Allocation Table 32-bit file system, widely compatible.
basic_answers: Old, compatible file system (USB drives).

definition: New Technology File System with security, journaling, and large volume support.
basic_answers: Windows file system — secure, supports big files.

definition: Fourth Extended File System, default for many Linux distributions.
basic_answers: Linux file system — fast, reliable.

definition: A group of interconnected computers that can exchange data and share resources.
basic_answers: Connected computers sharing data/resources.

definition: Local Area Network: covers small geographic area like office or home.
basic_answers: Network in one building (e.g., office Wi-Fi).

definition: Wide Area Network: spans large geographic areas, often using public networks.
basic_answers: Network across cities/countries (e.g., Internet).

definition: Metropolitan Area Network: covers a city or campus.
basic_answers: Network for a city (e.g., university campus).

definition: Personal Area Network: connects devices within an individual's workspace.
basic_answers: Your phone, laptop, smartwatch connected.

definition: 7-layer framework for understanding network communication.
basic_questions: What are the 7 layers of OSI?
basic_answers: 1. Physical, 2. Data Link, 3. Network, 4. Transport, 5. Session, 6. Presentation, 7. Application.

definition: 4-layer model used by the Internet: Application, Transport, Internet, Link.
basic_answers: 4 layers: Application, Transport, Internet, Link.

definition: Unique numerical label assigned to each device on a network.
basic_answers: Unique address for device on network (e.g., 192.168.1.1).

definition: 32-bit address format, e.g., 192.168.1.1, supports ~4.3 billion addresses.
basic_answers: 32-bit: 192.168.1.1 — ~4.3 billion addresses.

definition: 128-bit address format, e.g., 2001:0db8::1, supports vast address space.
basic_answers: 128-bit: 2001:db8::1 — huge address space.

definition: Hardware address burned into network interface card, unique globally.
basic_answers: Unique hardware ID: AA:BB:CC:11:22:33.

definition: Domain Name System: translates domain names to IP addresses.
basic_answers: Translates google.com → 142.250.190.14.

definition: HyperText Transfer Protocol: application protocol for web communication.
basic_answers: Protocol for web: browser ↔ server.

definition: HTTP Secure: encrypted version of HTTP using SSL/TLS.
basic_answers: Secure HTTP with encryption (SSL/TLS).

definition: File Transfer Protocol: standard for transferring files over networks.
basic_answers: Transfer files between computers.

definition: Simple Mail Transfer Protocol: for sending emails.
basic_answers: Sends emails (from your app to server).

definition: Transmission Control Protocol: reliable, connection-oriented, ensures delivery.
basic_answers: Reliable, ordered, connection-based (web, email).

definition: User Datagram Protocol: connectionless, fast, no guaranteed delivery.
basic_answers: Fast, no guarantee (video streaming, games).

definition: Logical endpoint for communication, identified by number (0–65535).
basic_answers: Number to identify service: 80 (HTTP), 443 (HTTPS).

definition: Security system that monitors and controls incoming/outgoing network traffic.
basic_answers: Blocks unauthorized traffic (security guard).

definition: Device that forwards data packets between computer networks.
basic_answers: Forwards packets between networks (e.g., home to ISP).

definition: Device that connects devices in a network by using MAC addresses.
basic_answers: Switch: same network (MAC). Router: different networks (IP).

definition: Basic networking device that broadcasts data to all connected devices.
basic_answers: Sends data to all devices (dumb, outdated).

definition: Used to divide IP address into network and host portions.
basic_answers: Separates network and host part: 255.255.255.0.

definition: Network Address Translation: maps private IPs to public IP for internet access.
basic_answers: Share one public IP with many private devices.

definition: Virtual Private Network: secure tunnel over public network.
basic_answers: Secure, private tunnel over internet.

definition: HyperText Markup Language: standard language for creating web pages.
basic_answers:
Structure of web pages:

,

,

.

definition: Cascading Style Sheets: language for styling HTML documents.
basic_answers: Styles HTML: color, font, layout.

definition: High-level programming language for adding interactivity to web pages.
basic_answers: Adds interactivity: click, animation, fetch data.

definition: Document Object Model: programming interface for HTML and XML documents.
basic_answers: Tree of HTML elements JS can manipulate.

definition: Web design approach that makes pages render well on all devices.
basic_answers: Website works on mobile, tablet, desktop.

definition: CSS layout model for one-dimensional layouts.
basic_answers: 1D layout: align items in row/column easily.

definition: CSS Grid Layout: two-dimensional layout system.
basic_answers: 2D layout: rows and columns.

definition: Popular CSS framework for responsive, mobile-first front-end development.
basic_answers: CSS framework with ready components (buttons, navbar).

definition: JavaScript library for building user interfaces with components.
basic_answers: Library for UI with reusable components.

definition: Progressive JavaScript framework for building UIs.
basic_answers: Lightweight framework for reactive UIs.

definition: Platform for building mobile and desktop web applications.
basic_answers: Full framework with two-way binding, TypeScript.

definition: JavaScript runtime for executing code outside the browser.
basic_answers: Run JavaScript on server (backend).

definition: Minimal web framework for Node.js.
basic_answers: Lightweight web server for Node.js.

definition: Architectural style for designing networked applications using HTTP methods.
basic_answers: API using HTTP: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE.

definition: Query language for APIs that enables clients to request specific data.
basic_answers: Query language: ask exactly what you need.

definition: JavaScript Object Notation: lightweight data interchange format.
basic_answers: Lightweight data format: { "name": "Ali" }

definition: Extensible Markup Language: markup language for encoding documents.
basic_answers: <person><name>Ali</name></person> — structured data.

definition: JSON Web Token: compact, URL-safe means of representing claims between parties.
basic_answers: Token for authentication: header.payload.signature

definition: Open standard for token-based authentication and authorization.
basic_answers: Login with Google/Facebook without password.

definition: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing: mechanism allowing restricted resources on a web page to be requested from another domain.
basic_answers: Allow frontend (localhost) to call API (server).

definition: Protocol providing full-duplex communication channels over a single TCP connection.
basic_answers: Real-time, two-way: chat, live updates.

definition: Field of AI where systems learn from data to make predictions or decisions.
basic_answers: Teach computers to learn from data, not rules.

definition: ML with labeled data: input → output mapping.
basic_answers: Learn from labeled data (input + correct output).

definition: ML with unlabeled data: finds patterns or clusters.
basic_answers: Find patterns in unlabeled data (e.g., clustering).

definition: Agent learns by trial and error, receiving rewards or penalties.
basic_answers: Learn by rewards/punishments (e.g., game AI).

definition: Predicts continuous values (e.g., house price).
basic_answers: Predict numbers (e.g., price, temperature).

definition: Predicts categorical labels (e.g., spam/not spam).
basic_answers: Predict categories (spam, cat/dog).

definition: Groups similar data points without labels (e.g., customer segmentation).
basic_answers: Group similar items (e.g., customer types).

definition: Unsupervised algorithm partitioning data into K clusters.
basic_answers: Divide data into K groups by nearest center.

definition: Tree-like model of decisions based on feature conditions.
basic_answers: If-else tree for classification/regression.

definition: Ensemble of decision trees using bagging.
basic_answers: Many decision trees vote together.

definition: Support Vector Machine: finds hyperplane maximizing margin between classes.
basic_answers: Draw line/plane to separate classes with max margin.

definition: K-Nearest Neighbors: classifies based on majority vote of nearest points.
basic_answers: Classify by majority of K nearest neighbors.

definition: Probabilistic classifier based on Bayes' theorem with independence assumption.
basic_answers: Spam filter using probability and independence.

definition: Computing system inspired by biological neural networks.
basic_answers: Layers of nodes (neurons) learning from data.

definition: Neural networks with many layers (deep architectures).
basic_answers: Neural nets with many hidden layers.

definition: Convolutional Neural Network: for image and video processing.
basic_answers: For images: detect edges, shapes, objects.

definition: Recurrent Neural Network: for sequential data like time series or text.
basic_answers: For sequences: text, speech, stock prices.

definition: Long Short-Term Memory: RNN variant that remembers long-term dependencies.
basic_answers: RNN that remembers long sequences.

definition: Architecture using self-attention, basis for models like BERT and GPT.
basic_answers: Attention-based model (BERT, GPT).

definition: Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers: pre-trained language model.
basic_answers: Pre-trained model understanding context both ways.

definition: Generative Pre-trained Transformer: large language model for text generation.
basic_answers: Generates human-like text (ChatGPT, Grok).

definition: Model learns training data too well, including noise, performs poorly on new data.
basic_answers: Model memorizes training data, fails on new data.

definition: Model is too simple to capture underlying patterns in data.
basic_answers: Model too simple, misses patterns.

definition: Techniques (L1, L2, dropout) to prevent overfitting.
basic_answers: Penalize complex models to avoid overfitting.

definition: Splitting data into folds to evaluate model performance robustly.
basic_answers: Split data into folds, train/test multiple times.

definition: Table showing True Positive, True Negative, False Positive, False Negative.
basic_answers: TP, TN, FP, FN in a table.

definition: TP / (TP + FP): accuracy of positive predictions.
basic_answers: Of predicted positives, how many are actually positive?

definition: TP / (TP + FN): ability to find all positive instances.
basic_answers: Of actual positives, how many did we catch?

definition: Harmonic mean of precision and recall.
basic_answers: Balance between precision and recall.

definition: Plot of True Positive Rate vs False Positive Rate at various thresholds.
basic_answers: Plot TPR vs FPR — higher curve = better model.

definition: Area Under the ROC Curve: measures model discrimination.
basic_answers: Area under ROC: 1.0 = perfect, 0.5 = random.

definition: Creating new features or transforming existing ones to improve model performance.
basic_answers: Create better input features for ML.

definition: Cleaning, normalizing, encoding, and scaling data before modeling.
basic_answers: Clean, scale, encode data before training.

definition: When classes are not represented equally in the dataset.
basic_answers: 99% negative, 1% positive → model ignores minority.

definition: Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique: generates synthetic samples for minority class.
basic_answers: Create fake minority samples to balance data.

definition: Open-source ML framework by Google for building and deploying models.
basic_answers: Google's ML framework (deep learning).

definition: Open-source ML library by Facebook with dynamic computation graphs.
basic_answers: Facebook's ML library — dynamic, researcher-friendly.

definition: Python library for classical ML algorithms and data preprocessing.
basic_answers: Classical ML: KNN, SVM, Random Forest.

definition: Python library for data manipulation and analysis using DataFrames.
basic_answers: Data analysis: DataFrames, CSV, Excel.

definition: Fundamental package for scientific computing with Python using arrays.
basic_answers: Fast arrays and math operations.

definition: Plotting library for creating static, animated, and interactive visualizations.
basic_answers: Plot graphs: line, bar, scatter.
